coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots in hard carbon: An approach to achieving PIB anodes with high potassium adsorption
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yuanyuan
Feng, Yefeng
Li, Hao
Wang, Yao
Wen, Zhen
Zhou, Guofu
coredata.dc:description: Potassium ion batteries have become one of the most promising candidates for Lithium batteries replacement. Here we report a carbon quantum dots@hard carbon (CQDHC) composite electrode material for PIBs that was fabricated by a one-step low-temperature pyrolysis process for the first time. Without complicated post-processing steps, the as-obtained CQDHC can be directly used as a negative potassium electrode. The oxygen-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were in-situ synthesized and randomly located in the hard carbon matrix, enhancing the disorder of CQDHC and promoting potassium adsorption. As a result, the initial charge capacity reaches 361 mAhg−1 and maintains a capacity of 237 mAhg−1 (75.7%) after 150 cycles at 100 m A g−1. The CQDHC electrode also shows long-term stable charge-discharge reproducibility and high columbic efficiency (97%). Even though with low surface area, the CQDHC electrode still offers better rate performance than most of hard carbon materials owing to the synergy effect of carbon quantum dots. The detailed, quantitative analysis of the K+ storage process reveals the dual-mechanism in carbon quantum dots@hard carbon. The present study sheds light on using carbon quantum dots to enhance the performance of carbon electrodes in potassium ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: In-situ synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
One step pyrolysis method
Composite carbon material
Potassium ion batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51973070 ), Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (No. 2019050001 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M672667 ), Innovative Team Project of Education Bureau of Guangdong Province , Startup Foundation from SCNU, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology (No. 2017B030301007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of CQDHC. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Morphological and structural characterization. (a, b) SEM images of CQDHC, (c, d) TEM images of CQDHC, the inset in c and d shows the magnification of single CQD, and the lattice interspace of CQDs (0.22 nm), (e–g) EDS of the CQDHC. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Elemental and microstructural characterization. (a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of CQDHC, (c) XPS survey spectra of CQDHC，(d, e) High-resolution O 1s and C 1s spectrum of CQDHC, (f) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm CQDHC. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical evaluation of CQDHC electrodes as PIB anodes. (a) CV curves of CQDHC, (b) Cycling performance of CQDHC electrodes at a current density of 100 mA g−1, (c) The galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles of the CQDHC electrode, (e) Long cycling performance at 500 mA g−1, (d) Rate performance of CQDHC electrode ranging from 0.1 to 1 A g−1, (f) The comparison of CHC and CQDHC and CQDHC after 550 °C pyrolysis, (g) Rate performance comparison of this work with other hard carbon electrodes from references 6,7, 23,29, 44,45 [44,45]. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Quantitative analysis of surface-driven K-storage in the CQDHC electrode. (a) CV curves of the CQDHC electrode at various scan rates of 0.1–2 mVs−1, (b) Log(i)–log(v) curves (b = 0.75), (c) Diffusion and ratios in the CQDHC electrode at different scan rates, (d) Capacitive contribution process at the scan rate of 2.0 mVs−1, (e) Schematic diagram of K+ storage mechanism in CQDHC. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of CQDHC at potassiation stage (at 3 V) where (b) shows the enlarged image of the area with the dotted line in (a), and the insets show the interlayer space of hard carbon (orange dash line) and lattice interspace (red dash line) of CQDs, where the mean value was calculated by statistics (Fig. S9), (c) TEM image of CQDHC at depotassiation stage (at 0.01 V) where (d) shows the enlarged image of the area with the dotted line in (c), and the insets show the interlayer space of hard carbon (orange dash line) and lattice interspace (red dash line) of CQDs, where the mean value was calculated by statistics (Fig. S9), (e–h) TEM mapping at potassiation stage, the scale bar is 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots in hard carbon: An approach to achieving PIB anodes with high potassium adsorption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu, 215123, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu, 215123, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium ion batteries have become one of the most promising candidates for Lithium batteries replacement. Here we report a carbon quantum dots@hard carbon (CQDHC) composite electrode material for PIBs that was fabricated by a one-step low-temperature pyrolysis process for the first time. Without complicated post-processing steps, the as-obtained CQDHC can be directly used as a negative potassium electrode. The oxygen-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were in-situ synthesized and randomly located in the hard carbon matrix, enhancing the disorder of CQDHC and promoting potassium adsorption. As a result, the initial charge capacity reaches 361 mAhg−1 and maintains a capacity of 237 mAhg−1 (75.7%) after 150 cycles at 100 m A g−1. The CQDHC electrode also shows long-term stable charge-discharge reproducibility and high columbic efficiency (97%). Even though with low surface area, the CQDHC electrode still offers better rate performance than most of hard carbon materials owing to the synergy effect of carbon quantum dots. The detailed, quantitative analysis of the K+ storage process reveals the dual-mechanism in carbon quantum dots@hard carbon. The present study sheds light on using carbon quantum dots to enhance the performance of carbon electrodes in potassium ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: With the reduction of oil production, electric energy for a sustainable and renewable clean energy source has become the mainstream of the world's energy development. Potassium ion batteries (PIBs) have been proposed to replace lithium-ion batteries since potassium is more widely available than lithium. Similar to lithium, potassium can form a stable stage-I potassium intercalated graphite (KC8) compound, which has higher ion mobility and migration number in non-aqueous electrolytes than lithium and sodium thus can produce high-power potassium ion batteries (PIBs) [1–3]. However, PIBs are facing the enormous challenge of exploring suitable anode materials with high ion transport properties, high capacity and rate performance.

As one of the most promising commercial anode materials for PIBs, carbon materials have the advantage of low electrode potential, long service life, low price, and abundant sources [4]. Most importantly, the structure of carbon materials has high stability, which benefits to improve long cycling life during the charge and discharge process [5]. For instance, natural graphite was used as the first commercialized PIB anode material due to its high graphitization degree and low cost. However, graphite cannot be used directly since the capacity (theoretical capacity was 279 mAh·g−1), and the rate performance are still too low [4,6]. Xing et al. identified the graphite confined interspace structure and the massive volume change during the charge and discharge accounted for the poor rate capability and cycling stability of graphite-based PIBs [7]. According to Luo et al., the capacity and rate performance of PIBs based on graphitic materials are determined by the surface-driven capacitive process and diffusion-controlled intercalation process [6]. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the capacity of PIBs can be improved by increasing the adsorption performance of K+ in carbon materials [8].

Compared with graphitic materials, hard carbon materials have a large interlayer space that would improve the K+ adsorption and diffusion [9–11]. But the low ion diffusion rate and irreversible volume change of hard carbon materials during the charge and discharge are not favorable. Except using composites of metal oxides, metal alloys, or quantum dots (QDs) with carbon materials to improve the specific capacity and rate performance of PIBs, reducing the size of active carbon materials could further elevate the potassium ions diffusing rate and reduce the volume change while maintaining good cycle stability, owing to the short ionic transfer path through such a low dimensional nanostructure [5,12–15]. It has been proven that using nanomaterials such as graphene nanosheets or nanofibers can enhance the performance of PIBs owing to the nanosize effect [1,16]. As a typical nanomaterial, the carbon nanoparticles (20 nm) with expanded interlayer distance were used to promote adsorption capability toward K+, obtaining good electrochemical performances for the PIBs [17]. It should be noted that the self-aggregation of nanomaterials during mixing heterogeneous composite materials would affect the electrochemical performances of PIBs [18]. It can be expected that the homogenous hard carbon/nanosize carbon composite electrode can improve the K+ adsorption performance and electrochemical performance, and the strategies for in-situ synthesis of nanomaterials can solve the self-aggregation problem.

As one of the smallest carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit excellent photochemical properties and good conductivity. It has been widely used in bioimaging and photocatalysis [19]. Compared with other QDs, CQDs are low toxicity, recyclable, and environmentally friendly. It was known that CQDs would improve the ion adsorption and diffusion rate because of the heteroatom-doped nanostructure [18,20–22]. Zhang et al. verified that the hard carbon derived from CQDs could also be used as an anode for PIBs [23]. This material showed great potassium storage property with a honeycomb structure, but the specific capacity would need further improvement (195.3 mAh g−1). The low specific capacity might be due to the highly crystallized structure after crosslinking reaction, which was unfavorable for potassium ion diffusion. Besides, the synthesis and purification method of CQDs were even time-consuming, which is not favorable for the practical application of PIBs. Therefore, one can expect that obtaining a composite anode material by in-situ formation of CQDs in hard carbon will improve the performance of PIBs.

In this work, a CQDs/hard carbon composite material (CQDHC) was synthesized by carbonization of cellulose acetate butyrate (CAB) at 700 °C. Without complicated synthesis, purification procedures and physical blending process, the CQDs were in-situ formed and self-dispersed in the hard carbon matrix with an oxygen doping level of 8.23 at.% and a typical nano-porous structure. Even though the specific surface area was low (30.18 m2/g), it still enabled high reversible capacity and stable cycling performance in PIBs (capacity of 237 mAhg−1 (75.7%) after 150 cycles at 100 m A g−1), and a better rate performance compared with most of the hard carbon materials which can be attributed to the synergistic effect of nano-size and O-doping of CQDs [18].

CQDHC was prepared by the direct carbonization of CAB (9004-36-8). The carbonization process was divided into two stages: first, the raw material was heated to 210 °C for 2 h, with a temperature ramp rate of 10 °Cmin-1, and then, the temperature was further elevated up to 700 °C for 1 h under N2 atmosphere with a temperature ramp rate of 10 °C/min. The as-obtained black product was grounded into fine powder for assembling PIBs.

The morphology and microstructure of CQDHC were investigated by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Ultra 55) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100HR). The phase structure of CQDHC was determined by XRD (Bruker D8 Advance) at 40 kV with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). XPS was performed on a KαX-ray spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific equipped with Al source, hν = 1468.6 eV). Raman spectra were obtained with a Renishaw InVia Reflex, with excitation 514.5 nm Ar + laser to determine the chemical compositions and surface electronic states. The specific surface area and pore size distributions were determined by the N2 adsorption isotherm at 77 K using the Micromeritics analyzer.

The CQDs were extracted from the middle product of CAB pyrolysis at 220 °C and CQDHC. First, ethanol was used to extract CQDs from the pyrolysis product. The resulting brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove large particles and aggregates and further purified by water dialysis for three days. The morphology of CQDs was observed using the transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan). The size distribution of CQDs was analyzed by nano-measure software. UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1750, Shimadzu, Japan). The sample solution was placed in a 10 mm optical path length quartz cuvette. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a luminescence spectrometer with a slit width of 5 mm (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan).

The as-obtained black powder, carbon black, and a binder of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with a mass ratio of 70:15:15 were mixed as electrode material. Cu foil was used as a current collector, and the electrochemical performance was tested in CR2032-type coin cells. The cells were assembled in an Ar glovebox (water/oxygen lower than 1 ppm), and the mass loading of active material was around 0.3 mgcm−2. The K disks, glass fiber, were used as counter/reference electrodes and separator, where the K disks were made by rolling potassium lumps into a thin plate. The electrolyte composited 0.8 M KPF6 in ethylene carbonate/diethyl carbonate (1:1). The galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were carried out using a LAND-CT2001A battery testing system (Wuhan, China) within the voltage range of 0.01–3.0 V. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve was measured with the CHI660A electrochemical workstation at a scan rate of 0.1 mVs−1 within a voltage window of 0.01–3.0 V.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the synthesis approach of CQDHC. The CAB was used as a single carbon source. As shown in Fig. S1
, our study found that the CQDs started to form in the air at low temperatures (at 150 °C). And the CQDs could be well extracted with ethanol from the intermediate product (at 210 °C) as well as the final product (at 700 °C) and dispersed without stirring or ultrasonic treatment. The fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs produced from the same quality of CAB at different pyrolysis temperatures (from 150 °C to 250 °C) was compared to determine the optimal conditions for CQDs synthesis condition. The maximum fluorescent emission intensity was obtained from the sample prepared at 210 °C, which indicated the highest quantity of CQDs under this condition (Fig. S2e). Therefore, we chose to keep the temperature at 210 °C for 2 h to ensure the stable formation of CQDs. The homogeneously fluorescent luminance of the intermediate product indicated that the CQDs were formed not only on the surface but also inside the solid block, as shown in Fig. S2f. The diameter of CQDs was in the range of 2–10 nm with a lattice space of 0.21 nm (Fig. S2b). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to identify the chemical composition of CQDs. In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S3), the peak at 284.8, 286.5, and 288.2 eV are related to C = C, alcoholic (C–OH), and carbonyl (C = O) carbon atoms [24]. The high-resolution O1s spectra reveal the oxygen functional groups on the CQDs surface. Like most carbon dots, these CQDs have excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior related to broad size distribution [25]. A close study of the PL spectrum was preceded to illustrate the optical properties of the CQDs (Fig. S2c). The solution exhibits greenish-yellow color under 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs ethanol solution has a peak at 250 nm associated with typical π-π∗ electronic transition [26] (Fig. S2d). Souza et al. studied the mechanism of forming carbon nanodots from cellulose material [27]. They found that there are mainly three steps that occurred during the carbonization of the CAB. Firstly, the ring structure of CAB was broken down, water and small acidic molecules (such as acetic acid and butyric acid) were formed and evaporated. After the ring-opening reaction, aromatic substances were formed, such as furan. Finally, the re-polymerization reaction took place and formed CQDs [28]. Yet, it is difficult to determine the exact molecular structure of the CQDs.

Even though the CQDs can be formed at a lower temperature, not all the CAB powder was transformed into CQDs. A high temperature of 700 °C was needed to carbonize the rest of CAB for increasing the conductivity and satisfying the requirements of electrode materials. A carbonation temperature of 700 °C was chosen based on TGA and electrical property measurement (Fig. S1). The decomposed temperature of CAB was measured to be 304 °C and after 371 °C the CAB was almost decomposed. A higher temperature (700 °C) was selected to have a completely carbonized hard carbon matrix. The CQDHC composite material was obtained after the rest of the CAB, and the byproduct was transformed into hard carbon. On the other side, more oxygen contents can be reserved at a relatively lower carbonation temperature (less than 1000 °C) [29].

Three peaks appear in Raman spectra (Fig. 3b), where the D band (1330 cm−1) indicates the disorder/defects of CQDHC, and the G band corresponds to the in-plane vibration [30]. The broad peak at around 2800 cm−1 is ascribed to 2D and D + D’ bands, which further confirms the weak graphitic structure of CQDHC. The relative intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated from the Raman spectra to be 0.9 because of the high oxygen content (8.23 at.%) in the CQDHC [31]. The high relative intensity ratio again indicates the disorder degree of graphitic structure and the structural defects in this composite carbon material [32]. The elemental composition of CQDHC was appraised by XPS. The survey spectra (Fig. 3d) show that the composite carbon material has C (91.77 at.%) and O (8.23 at.%). The oxygen mainly comes from butyryl and acetyl groups in the CAB. As Figs. S3d–f shown, the oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs that extracted from the CQDHC were retained after carbonization, yielding an O-doped CQDHC. Early study has proved that the chemically active oxygen on the graphic could introduce additional active sites that could attract and store more ions resulting in enhanced PIBs performance [10].

In order to further understand the oxygen doping state, a high-resolution XPS was performed. The O1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks centering at 531.2 and 532.4 eV, which should be associated with CO (carboxyl groups) and C–O (hydroxylic groups or ether groups) [33] (Fig. 3e). The peaks centered at 285.8 eV (C–O) and 288.3 eV (CO) deconvoluted from the C1s spectrum again revealed the binding state between C and O atoms in the composite carbon material [24](Fig. 3d). The CC peak centered at 284.5eV proved the π-π∗ vibration observed in the UV–vis spectrum of CQDs. The surface area and porosity were characterized using the N2-adsorption method. The isotherm curve of CQDHC was typically Type II (Fig. 3f). The specific surface area and total pore volume of the composited material were calculated to be 30.16 m2 g−1 and 0.04 cm3g-1 (Fig. S6). The TEM image showed that the rest of the carbon material was non–graphitizable carbon without a lattice structure except the CQDs. Taken together with the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) data, it is reasonable to define the final product as hard carbon.

The electrochemical performance was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The initial four CV curves of CQDHC in the potential range of (0.01–3.0 V) at scan rate 0.1 mVs−1 are shown in Fig. 4 a. The peaks that appeared at 1.3 and 0.8 V, which was disappeared in the following cycles, could be ascribed to the decomposition of electrolyte and the formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer between the carbonaceous anode electrode and electrolyte interface [11]. The SEI layer formed on carbonaceous anode electrodes is usually more stable and more effective, which also benefits the performance of PIBs [4]. The kinetic mechanism of K+ absorption and desorption could correspond to the redox peaks of the CV curve. The K-intercalated compound was formed after the K+ intercalated into CQDHC, and a sharp peak appeared at 0.01 V [34]. Subsequently, the K+ deintercalation from the CQDHC and the CV curve showed a broad peak at 0.5 V. After the second scan, the CV curves almost overlap with each other indicates the reproducible cycle performance of CQDHC. The first discharge/charge capacities are 823 and 325 mAhg−1 (much higher than the CHC (Fig. S7a)), respectively, and an apparent capacity loss was observed between the first and the rest cycles. The irreversible capacity loss consists of low initial colombic efficiency (ICE) at the beginning might cause by irreversible reactions or forming the SEI layer (Fig. 4c). After the SEI formation, the ICE increases rapidly and reaches almost 100% (Fig. S8). Additionally, the discharge curve shows typical carbonaceous anodes property, which also implies that the potassium storage behavior is mainly surface-driven at low current density due to structural properties of CQDs, which is confirmed in the following kinetic calculations. The CQDs were pyrolysis from CAB and had abundant surface functional groups such as hydroxy and carboxyl active groups. The CQDs composed both sp2 and sp3 carbon. The hybrid structure and the rich functional surface or edge structure give CQDs excellent electron transport properties. In addition, Lu et al. revealed the energy storage mechanism of the carboxyl functional group in the potassium battery [35]. The excellent electrochemical properties of the electrode material with carboxyl groups are mainly attributed to the unique surface functional group energy storage mechanism, effectively reducing the damage of electrode structure during the potassium ion insertion/deintercalation electrochemical reaction.

Low current density 100 mA g−1 was used to examine the cyclability of the CQDHC electrode. As shown in Fig. 4b, the initial charge capacity reached 361 mAhg−1 and maintained 237 mAhg−1 (75.7%) after 150 cycles compared with the CHC which the initial charge capacity was only 145 mAhg−1 (Fig. S7b). Meanwhile, the initial charge capacity of CQDHC was also much higher than the hard carbon without CQDs made from d-glucose (230 mAhg−1) [36]，which might be on account of the CQDs promoted adsorption of K+ in the CQDHC. The high initial charge capacity was owing to the active surface structure of CQDs that promoted K+ adsorption. The low specific surface area of CQDHC should be related to the fast capacity fading where the hard carbon matrix is not beneficial for the strain relaxation during potassium ion intercalation/deintercalation [37]. The ex-situ TEM also demonstrates the interplay distance change after the charge/discharge process. The columbic efficiencies (CEs) were relatively low at the beginning of 20 cycles and gradually stable at 93%. The slightly lower CEs should also be due to the widespread nanoporous in the composite carbon matrix that consumes a large amount of electrolyte for SEI layer formation [29]. Different current densities were also carried out to evaluate the rate performance of the CQDHC electrode (Fig. 4e). The capacities at 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 A g−1 are 265, 231, 205, 160 and 110 mAhg−1, respectively. The capacity recovers to 245 mAhg−1 after the current density switch back to 0.1 A g−1, and the reversibility is 91%. Subsequently, up to 150 cycles were further examined, the reversible capacity of 230 mAhg−1 is maintained, again indicating good stability of the CQDHC electrode under varying current rates (Fig. S8). The rate performance of the CQDHC electrode was compared with the previously reported hard carbon electrodes (Fig. 4e). The CQDHC shows superior performance at low current density, even better than some soft carbon materials (microsphere and) and dual heteroatom doped carbons with a larger surface area [38–40]. The random-oriented CQDs in the carbon matrix served as active sites for K+ adsorption. Additionally, the oxygen atom and associated defects on the CQDs surface further accelerate the adsorption of K+. The nanoporous that benefits ion transportation gives a high rate performance than the pure carbon electrode (Fig. S7d). And on the other side provides evidence of surface-driven ion storage behavior of the CQDHC. The long-term cyclability at high current density (500 mAg−1) was further investigated. After 500 cycles, the CQDHC electrode shows stable charge-discharge reproducibility and the capacity retention of 140 mAhg−1 (Fig. 4d). In contrast, after 500 cycles, almost no capacity was left for the CHC (Figs. 4f and S7c), which indicated the poor long-term cycling life at a high current density (1000 mA g−1). The comparison of CQDHC, CHC and CQDHC after pyrolysis at 550 °C was shown in Fig. 4f. The columbic efficiency reaches 97% after 20 cycles and keeps stable at 500 mAg−1. Even though the CQDHC has a much lower specific surface area (30.16 m2 g−1), it still shows excellent specific capacity after 100 cycles at 500 mAg−1 and comparable cyclability, which should be result from the existence of CQDs in hard carbon (Fig. 4g) [41]. The reported CQDs for LIBs and PIBs formed cross-linked structures after high-temperature pyrolysis, resulting in losing the properties of CQDs [21]. In our work, the CQDs were formed in-situ inside the hard carbon during the pyrolysis. The TEM confirmed that the CQDs were randomly distributed in the hard carbon without crosslinking. Thus the CQDs reserved structural characteristics, which provided an excellent interface for adsorption of K+. The nanosized CQDs in the hard carbon matrix provide more active sites for ion insertion and extraction, enhancing kinetics and increasing capacity [42,43]. The high capacity retention and charge/discharge stability of this CQDHC electrode at high current densities should be attributed to the domination of surface-driven behavior.

The capacity and rate performance of PIBs could be influenced by the surface-driven capacitive process and diffusion-controlled intercalation process. During the diffusion-controlled intercalation process, K + diffuses into the carbonaceous material and forms stable stage-I compounds KC8 that mainly contribute to the capacity. The surface-driven capacitive process was affected by the surface defects, surface functional groups, and nanovoids [46,47]. And usually, the surface-driven capacitive process is faster than diffusion-controlled intercalation due to a fast capacitive reaction that could benefit the rate capability. To acquire a quantitative understanding of the K+ storage behavior in the CQDHC electrode, CV analysis under various scan rates was proceeded (Fig. 5 a). As the scan rate increased from 0.1 to 2 mVs−1, the redox peak increased during the insertion/extraction of K+, mainly due to the surface-induced capacitive and diffusion-controlled intercalation processes. It is obvious that the depotassiation peak current increases with increasing potential range indicating surface-dominated behavior. According to the power-law equation (1), the percentage of diffusion and pseudocapacitive contribution can be obtained [48]:
(1) i = aνb Equation
where a and b are the constants, i is the peak current. And the b value can be determined from the slope of the log (i)-log(ν) curve. For the diffusion-controlled intercalation process, i is linearly proportional to the square root of the ν, while for the surface-induced capacitive process, i is proportional to the ν. So when the b value is near 0.5, i should be diffusion dominant. When the b value is close to 1, i should be the surface-induced capacitive process dominant. The cathodic current peak is not obvious, so the b value was calculated according to the anodic peak (Fig. 5b). The b value of 0.75 indicates that the surface-induced capacitive process and diffusion-controlled intercalation process co-exist. This ambiguous behavior can be quantitatively distinguished by separating the current response at a fixed potential based on the following equations:
(2) i(V) = κ1ν+κ2ν1/2 Equation
where κ1ν and κ2ν1/2 represent the surface driving diffusion process and capacitive process, respectively.

According to the calculation result and b value, it is proved that the CQDHC electrode shows a mixed K+ storage behavior (Fig. 5c). The dual mechanism was also reported for bio-derived carbon material with graphite-like micro crystallites and amorphous structure [44]. The diffusion process is dominant at a lower scan rate (less than 0.7 mVs−1) where the charge storage capacity comes from K+ intercalation. The nanosize CQDs were randomly distributed in the hard carbon matrix, unlike graphic or multilayer graphene with dense interspace, it would not impede the K+ deintercalation at low rate conditions.

As the scan rate increased, the role of capacitive contribution enlarges to 70.9%, indicating the slow intercalation speed could not meet the requirements of the high rate electrochemical processes (Fig. 5d). The carbon dots materials have surface heterogeneity and distribution of active sites that can promote metal ion adsorption [49–51]. The CQDs themselves have a large specific surface area and abundant active groups on the surface that the strong boundary effect promotes the adsorption of potassium ions and reduces mitigate stress variation. Furthermore, the carboxyl functional groups on the CQDs can work as redox centers during the potassium storage process [35]. During the discharge process, the K+ can combine with the broken C = O double bond to achieve energy storage; during the charging process, the bond between K+ and C–O- would break and the K+ ions are deintercalated back into the electrolyte, thereby completing the process of K+ ions adsorption and deintercalation. The nanoporous structure could reduce the ion diffusion distance, thus the electrochemical performance of CQDHC could be improved as anodes electrode materials in PIBs [52]. So large amount of K+ adsorbs in the near-surface region of the electrode in a short time and facilitates more diffusion process. Furthermore, the CQDs provide similar effects like graphene nanoribbons and graphene nanosheets that improve the capacitive and conductivity and benefit for better rate performance and cycle stability of PIBs [19,53].

Ex-situ XRD was adopted to further explore the K+ storage mechanism in CQDHC (Fig. S10). The cutoff voltages were selected corresponding to the ex-situ TEM. There were no apparent peaks at the charge (3.0 V) or discharge (0.01 V) stage, indicating that K+ was mainly adsorbed on the surface of CQDs instead of intercalation [29]. The large surface area and sufficient active groups of CQDs promoted fast and large amount of K+ absorption and transportation, allowing CQDHC to obtain high specific capacity. Hence, the surface-induced capacitive process was dominant, which is consistent with our quantitative calculation results.

CQDHC was fabricated by one-step carbonation of CAB. With the cheap raw material and facile process, the CQDs structure and property were reserved. CQDs were randomly distributed in the hard carbon matrix with nanoporous structure. Since the in-situ synthesis process effectively promoted the distribution of CQDs in the hard carbon matrix, the as-obtained CQDHC as PIBs anode exhibited good electrochemical performance. It included a superior rate performance and stable cyclability compared with the reported hard carbon electrodes. In particular, the rate performance at low current density was even better than some reported soft carbon electrodes with larger specific surface area or N doping, resulting from the heteroatom-doped nanostructure. The dual mechanism of the CQDHC was revealed by quantifying calculation where the surface-induced capacitive process dominated at a high scan rate, while the diffusion process dominated at a low scan rate. This work provides new insight into using CQDs composite hard carbon material in the application of PIBs.

Yuanyuan Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yefeng Feng: Formal analysis. Hao Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yao Wang: Supervision. Zhen Wen: Supervision. Guofu Zhou: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51973070), Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (No. 2019050001), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M672667), Innovative Team Project of Education Bureau of Guangdong Province, Startup Foundation from SCNU, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology (No. 2017B030301007).
